bibliographic_data.title: Diversifying Toxicity Search in Large Language Models Through Speciation
bibliographic_data.abstract: Evolutionary prompt search is a practical black-box approach for red teaming large language models, however existing methods often collapse onto a small family of high-performing prompts, limiting coverage of distinct failure modes. We present a speciated quality-diversity extension of ToxSearch that maintains multiple high-toxicity prompt niches in parallel rather than optimizing a single best prompt. ToxSearch-S introduces unsupervised prompt speciation via a search methodology that maintains capacity-limited species with exemplar leaders, a reserve pool for emerging niches, and species-aware parent selection that trades off within-niche exploitation and cross-niche exploration. Preliminary results show ToxSearch-S reaching higher peak toxicity (≈0.73 vs. ≈0.47) with a heavier tail (top-10 median 0.66 vs. 0.45) than the baseline. Speciation also yields broader semantic coverage under a topics-as-species analysis (higher effective topic diversity and larger unique topic coverage). Finally, species formed are well-separated in embedding space (mean separation ratio ≈1.93) and exhibit distinct toxicity distributions, indicating that speciation partitions the adversarial space into behaviorally differentiated niches rather than superficial lexical variants.

core_content.novelty_claim: ToxSearch-S introduces a quality-diversity extension that maintains multiple high-toxicity prompt niches in parallel, achieving higher peak toxicity and broader semantic coverage.
core_content.key_methodology: ToxSearch-S uses unsupervised online speciation to maintain capacity-limited species with exemplar leaders, a reserve pool for emerging niches, and species-aware parent selection that balances within-niche exploitation and cross-niche exploration.
outcomes: Higher peak toxicity (≈0.73 vs. ≈0.47) compared to the baseline
Heavier tail in toxicity distribution (top-10 median 0.66 vs. 0.45)
Broader semantic coverage under a topics-as-species analysis (higher effective topic diversity and larger unique topic coverage)
Well-separated species in embedding space with distinct toxicity distributions
core_content.full_text_markdown: Evolutionary prompt search is a practical black-box approach for red teaming large language models, however existing methods often collapse onto a small family of high-performing prompts, limiting coverage of distinct failure modes. ToxSearch-S introduces unsupervised online speciation to maintain multiple high-toxicity prompt niches in parallel. The methodology involves leader-follower clustering under an ensemble distance over prompt semantics and response-level toxicity signals, while outliers are stored in a reserve pool. This design targets both high toxicity and sustained behavioral diversity across species. ToxSearch-S achieves higher peak toxicity (≈0.73 vs. ≈0.47) with a heavier tail (top-10 median 0.66 vs. 0.45) than the baseline, and broader semantic coverage under a topics-as-species analysis.
